our_argument_summary: Our review says the pre-1990 linear drift of the North Magnetic Pole is non-discriminating: both the dome model and the standard model fit the same NOAA data, and the dome provides no distinct mechanism or predicted drift rate. We cite roughly 9 km/yr before 1990.

relevant_files: monitor.py — NMP Longitude domain (~line 3), NMP Drift Rate domain (~line 11)
inject_ai_layer.py — WIN-006 listed as confirmed, 0.0466 deg/yr
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py fetches NP.xy from https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy, parses (lon, lat, year) tuples, then tests two things: (1) current NMP longitude > 130°E, and (2) current drift rate vs formula rate = 55×exp(-0.08×(year-2015)) km/yr. Neither domain validates the pre-1990 linearity claim. WIN-006 is listed as 'confirmed' in inject_ai_layer.py based on the assertion that the NOAA data shows 0.0466 deg/yr linear drift from 1590-1990, but no script in the repository computes or validates this linearity.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The 0.0466 deg/yr figure is hardcoded text in inject_ai_layer.py and the website HTML. No script in the repository performs a linear regression on the 1590-1990 NP.xy data. The current NMP drift rate formula (55×exp(-0.08×(yr-2015))) is computed dynamically but pertains to the post-1990 period, not WIN-006.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The NMP Drift Rate domain uses an adaptive tolerance with a floor of 50%, which is extremely generous. For 2025, the model predicts 24.7 km/yr vs actual ~40 km/yr (38% error), which still passes under the 50% tolerance.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review correctly states the dome offers no equations or predicted drift rate for the pre-1990 period. The code confirms this — there is no pre-1990 validation routine.

holes_found.description: Our review states 'roughly 9 km per year before 1990.' This is the long-term 1900-1990 displacement average (9.7 km/yr endpoint-to-endpoint). However, the actual decade-by-decade rates in the 1975-1990 period were 12-18 km/yr, and the scientific literature (EarthSky, citing Livermore & Finlay 2020) states 'up to about 15 km/yr' before the 1990s. Our '9 km/yr' understates the late pre-1990 drift speed.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'roughly 9 km per year' to 'roughly 10-15 km per year' or 'up to about 15 km per year (NOAA historical records)' to match both the data and the published literature.

holes_found.description: Our review doesn't challenge the dome's specific claim of 0.0466 deg/yr. This number is not reproducible from the NOAA NP.xy data using any standard metric: great-circle angular rate gives 0.0115 deg/yr (endpoint) or 0.0099 deg/yr (regression slope); longitude regression gives 0.038 deg/yr; naive Euclidean gives 0.034 deg/yr; cumulative path gives ~0.06 deg/yr. The provenance of 0.0466 is unclear.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a note that the dome's claimed 0.0466 deg/yr rate cannot be independently reproduced from the NOAA NP.xy data. This strengthens the 'no prior prediction' argument by showing the claimed observation metric is itself unverifiable.

stronger_arguments.description: The pre-1990 North Magnetic Pole trajectory is NOT linear by any reasonable definition. Between 1590 and 1990, the pole reverses direction at least 3 times in longitude and at least twice in latitude. From 1590-1632 it moves north, 1632-1730 south, then generally north again with oscillations. A linear regression of displacement-from-origin vs. year gives R² = 0.44 — barely correlated. The dome's claim of 'linear drift' is a post-hoc narrative imposed on wandering, non-monotonic data. This is stronger than our current argument because it attacks the premise rather than just saying 'both models explain it.'
sources: R² = 0.44 for displacement vs time regression

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's monitoring code (monitor.py) doesn't actually validate WIN-006. The two NMP-related domains test only current longitude (>130°E) and current drift rate. No script performs a linearity test on the 1590-1990 data. Meanwhile, the standard model explains the historical pole trajectory through specific physical mechanisms: Livermore & Finlay (2020, Nature Geoscience) showed that two competing flux lobes on the core-mantle boundary (under Canada and Siberia) determine pole position, with the elongation of the Canadian lobe causing the post-1990 acceleration. This mechanism also explains the pre-1990 quasi-steady behavior as a period when the Canadian lobe was dominant and stable.
sources: Livermore, Finlay & Bayliff (2020), 'Recent north magnetic pole acceleration towards Siberia caused by flux lobe elongation', Nature Geoscience 13, 387-391

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's current drift rate model (55×exp(-0.08×(yr-2015))) already fails for 2025: it predicts 24.7 km/yr vs observed ~40 km/yr (38% error). It only passes because the tolerance floor is 50%. The model even includes a built-in excuse: the explain_failure function suggests 'Deceleration constant (0.08) may need refinement to ~0.065' — pre-writing its own parameter adjustment excuse. This is relevant to WIN-006 because it shows the dome has no predictive model of pole drift at all, just post-hoc curve fitting with generous tolerances.
sources: monitor.py source code, NMP Drift Rate domain

kernel_of_truth: The North Magnetic Pole did exhibit a notably different character of motion before vs. after ~1990. The pre-1990 period showed relatively slow, meandering drift (5-15 km/yr average depending on timeframe), while the post-1990 period showed rapid, directed motion toward Siberia (peaking at ~55 km/yr around 2005). This contrast is real and scientifically significant. The dome model is correct that something changed, and it's not unreasonable to look for a physical phase transition. The standard model's explanation (Livermore & Finlay 2020) attributes this to a change in core flow patterns beneath Canada that elongated and weakened the Canadian flux lobe. This is itself a kind of transition in the underlying dynamics, which the dome model vaguely gestures at with 'aetheric vortex threshold crossing' — but without any mathematical framework, predicted timing, or predicted magnitude.
deeper_analysis:
WIN-006 claims that North Pole drift from 1590-1990 follows a linear trend, citing 0.0466 deg/yr from the NOAA NP.xy dataset. Our review correctly identifies this as non-discriminating but misses two opportunities to strengthen the argument.

First, the characterization of the pre-1990 drift as 'linear' is demonstrably false. Analyzing the full NOAA NP.xy record (436 annual data points, 1590-2025), the NMP trajectory between 1590-1990 is profoundly non-linear. The pole reverses direction in latitude at least twice (northward 1590-1632, southward 1632-1730, then northward again) and in longitude at least three times. A linear regression of great-circle displacement from the 1590 starting position versus time yields R² = 0.44, indicating the linear model explains less than half the variance. The dome's claimed rate of 0.0466 deg/yr cannot be reproduced from the NOAA data using any standard metric: great-circle angular displacement rate gives 0.010-0.012 deg/yr, longitude regression gives 0.038 deg/yr, naive Euclidean displacement gives 0.034 deg/yr. The number's provenance is undocumented.

Second, the dome model's own codebase reveals that WIN-006 is never computationally validated. The monitor.py audit engine defines two NMP-related domains: 'NMP Longitude' (testing whether current longitude exceeds 130°E) and 'NMP Drift Rate' (testing current rate against the formula 55×exp(-0.08×(year-2015)) km/yr with a 50% tolerance floor). Neither domain performs any historical linearity analysis. The 'confirmed' status of WIN-006 derives entirely from a textual assertion in inject_ai_layer.py, not from any computation in the repository. This is consistent with the broader pattern identified in our review where 'confirmed' status is asserted rather than computed.

Third, our stated pre-1990 drift rate of 'roughly 9 km per year' is an underestimate for the late pre-1990 period. While the long-term 1900-1990 endpoint displacement rate is indeed ~9.7 km/yr, the decade-by-decade rates were 12-18 km/yr in the 1975-1990 period. The scientific literature (Livermore & Finlay 2020, Nature Geoscience) and popular summaries (EarthSky) cite 'up to about 15 km/yr' before the 1990s. This minor inaccuracy doesn't affect our verdict but could be used by a dome defender to question our precision.

The kernel of truth here is real and worth acknowledging: there genuinely is a contrast between pre-1990 and post-1990 NMP behavior. But the standard model provides a specific, testable mechanism for this contrast — the Livermore & Finlay (2020) study using Swarm satellite data identified two competing magnetic flux lobes at the core-mantle boundary (under Canada and Siberia) and showed that elongation of the Canadian lobe, driven by changing core flow patterns between 1970-1999, weakened its surface expression and shifted the pole toward Siberia. The dome model offers only 'aetheric vortex dynamics' with no equations, no predicted timing, and no predicted rate — making WIN-006 a textbook example of a non-discriminating observation claimed as a prediction.
